\end{tabularx}
\end{table}

\subsection{How Participants Read the States}
The open responses explain the recognition scores. Participants reported reading the two channels together rather than in isolation, using pulse speed for activation and the ring for control, which is what the compositional design intended, and several described weighing one cue against the other before answering. As P12 put it, ``The combination worked better than either cue alone. Pulse speed gave the intensity, and the ring showed whether it was controlled.'' The pulsing centre and the ring's degree of fragmentation were named repeatedly as the features that carried the most meaning. The states participants found hardest were the adjacent middle levels on each scale, and the closest pair by a wide margin was the stable and reduced variability states, which matches the neighbour confusions concentrated in the recognition data; P4 described exactly this: ``V2 and V3 were the closest pair for me. I could see that the ring changed, but not always enough to choose confidently.''

\subsection{Usefulness, Boundaries, and Willingness to Use}
Participants judged the display useful for cooperative play, and several tied that judgement directly to context and control rather than to the visual design alone; P13 said the information ``could be genuinely helpful during difficult sections of a game. It becomes invasive only if teammates or spectators can access it automatically.'' The same responses set conditions. Participants asked for control over who can see their status, for the display to stay optional and mutable, and for consent to be built into the default rather than added later, and some described continuous live body data as inherently personal, with P4 willing to accept it ``only with clear consent and an easy off switch.'' Willingness to adopt was expressed most often for cooperative and squad modes where a teammate cannot pause to explain their state, as P2 put it: ``Probably yes. It could replace some voice-chat explanations during intense moments, as long as it stays optional.''

\section{Study 2 Methodology: Live Multiplayer Evaluation of Physiological Awareness Cues}
\label{sec:study2-methods}
\noindent
To evaluate whether the architecture proposed in Section~\ref{sec:study1-methods} supports coordination during actual cooperative play, we designed a second study following the online icon-legibility validation reported above. Whereas Study 1 tested whether participants could interpret the HR/HRV icon system in isolation, Study 2 tests whether live physiological cues, rendered by an implemented multiplayer prototype, improve teammate awareness, support behavior, and coordination inside a cooperative task. This section reports Study 2's design, apparatus, and procedure in full; its behavioral, questionnaire, and communication-coding results were still being collected and analyzed at the time of writing (Section~\ref{sec:study2-results}).

\subsection{How Study 2 Extends Study 1}
Study 1 established that the icon system communicates a teammate's HR and HRV state at a rate well above chance, using scripted states shown in a single-avatar browser prototype and outcomes of recognition accuracy, affective rating, and workload. Study 2 keeps that visual language untouched and moves the evaluation into live cooperative gameplay, asking whether live physiological visibility changes teammate awareness, support timing, coordination behavior, workload, and privacy perception during a multiplayer task, and whether the implemented wearable-to-interface pipeline itself operates reliably enough for live play.

\subsection{Research Questions and Hypotheses}
Study 2 addressed four research questions: whether a live HR/HRV avatar cue improves remote teammates' awareness of one another during cooperative gameplay (RQ2.1); whether it improves support timing and coordination relative to no cue and to a numeric physiological display (RQ2.2); whether an ambient animated cue feels less invasive and more usable than explicit numeric disclosure (RQ2.3); and whether the implemented wearable-to-interface pipeline operates reliably enough for live cooperative play (RQ2.4).

We set six hypotheses, distinct from and building on the H1--H5 of Study 1. \textbf{H2.1 (Awareness):} participants report higher teammate awareness in the Icon Cue condition than in the No Cue condition. \textbf{H2.2 (Coordination usefulness):} participants rate the Icon Cue condition as more useful for coordination than both the No Cue and Numeric Cue conditions. \textbf{H2.3 (Support response):} teams respond faster to scripted teammate-pressure events in the Icon Cue condition than in the No Cue condition. \textbf{H2.4 (Privacy/invasiveness):} the Numeric Cue condition is rated as more invasive than the Icon Cue condition. \textbf{H2.5 (Workload):} the Icon Cue condition does not produce higher workload than the Numeric Cue condition and remains at or below the midpoint of the workload scale. \textbf{H2.6 (Communication pattern):} the Icon Cue condition reduces general uncertainty and status-check communication while increasing targeted support behavior.

\subsection{Gameplay Task and Physiological Apparatus}
The gameplay task is modeled on Overcooked-style cooperative cooking, in which players deliver orders by collecting, preparing, and cooking ingredients, then plating and delivering the finished dish before time expires, splitting tasks on the fly and adapting roles under time pressure \cite{carroll_utility_2019, humancompatibleai_overcookedai}. Overcooked-based tasks have supported human-AI coordination research \cite{carroll_utility_2019} and multiplayer user studies of role division and shared strategy \cite{baek_toward_2022}; Overcooked! 2 itself officially supports up to four simultaneous players \cite{team17_overcooked_2018}. We adapted this structure rather than using copyrighted commercial levels, building a custom BioTerrarium-themed task that preserves the core action chain of ingredient collection, preparation, reactor cooking, plating, and delivery, so that team performance, role division, and moments of teammate overload remain measurable and comparable across conditions.

Study 2 implements the physiological pipeline that Study 1 left as a proposed architecture. Before gameplay, each participant wore a physiological wearable device and completed a three-minute seated baseline calibration, during which the system computed personal resting HR and HRV. During gameplay, live HR and HRV values were classified relative to each participant's own baseline into the same four HR states and four HRV states validated in Study 1 (Tables~\ref{tab:hr-stimulus-classification} and~\ref{tab:hrv-stimulus-classification}), rather than against an absolute clinical threshold, and a state change had to persist across consecutive display windows before the visible cue updated, to limit flicker. The system logged raw physiological values, personal baselines, classified HR/HRV states, displayed interface states, timestamps, missing-data events, and sensor-to-interface latency, which together speak to RQ2.4.

\subsection{Participants and Team Size}
We recruited 36 adult participants, organized as 12 teams of three players in soft Prep, Cook, and Runner roles, rather than 9 teams of four, even though Overcooked! 2 officially supports four-player sessions \cite{team17_overcooked_2018}. Three-player teams were chosen because two-player designs already anchor the Overcooked coordination paradigm of task splitting and role adaptation \cite{carroll_utility_2019}, because team-size research finds that additional members increase coordination overhead \cite{hoegl_smaller_2005, straub_cost_2023} and a fourth player would double the pairwise communication channels relative to three, and because 12 teams provide more independent team-level units for the team-by-condition analyses below than 9 teams would. Roles were introduced during training but remained flexible: players could switch tasks during a mission, and role switches were logged as strategy-change events rather than protocol violations.

\subsection{Interface Conditions and Counterbalancing}
Each team completed three seven-minute missions, one under each of three interface conditions: a No Cue baseline showing only the standard game interface; a Numeric Cue condition showing teammates' HR and HRV as explicit numerical or categorical values; and an Icon Cue condition showing the animated HR/HRV avatar cue validated in Study 1, in which heart rate controls pulse speed, glow, brightness, and halo expansion, and heart-rate variability controls ring coherence, segmentation, jitter, and stability. Condition order was counterbalanced with a Latin-square schedule across three groups of four teams, so that every condition appeared in every serial position, and the three mission layouts described below were rotated across condition orders so that no condition was systematically paired with an easier or harder layout.

\subsection{Mission Layouts and Scripted Pressure Events}
The three mission layouts reproduce common Overcooked-style cooperation patterns: a shared congestion kitchen with clustered resources and collision risk, a divided pass kitchen requiring mandatory item passing between zones, and a bottleneck-route kitchen where solo routing is inefficient and temporary support pays off. Each seven-minute mission also included three scripted pressure events, controlled coordination probes rather than random obstacles, each overloading a different role while the other two players had incomplete information about it (Table~\ref{tab:pressure-events}).

\begin{table*}[t]
\centering
\caption{Scripted teammate-pressure events used in every Study 2 mission. Each event overloads one role while the other two players hold incomplete information about it.}
\label{tab:pressure-events}
\footnotesize
\setlength{\tabcolsep}{5pt}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.15}
\begin{tabularx}{\textwidth}{@{}p{0.20\textwidth}YYY@{}}
\toprule
\textbf{Event} & \textbf{Trigger} & \textbf{Overloaded role} & \textbf{Primary measure} \\
\midrule
\textbf{Ingredient Bottleneck} & Around 2:00, two active orders require the same ingredient type. & Prep & Support response time from event onset. \\
\textbf{Pot Overload} & Around 4:30, two reactor pots become active close together. & Cook & Recovery time and burned-dish prevention. \\
\textbf{Service Rush} & Near the final phase, completed dishes, dirty plates, and delivery tasks accumulate together. & Runner & Appropriate support actions within 10 seconds. \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabularx}
\end{table*}

\subsection{Procedure}
Each session opened with consent and a demographic questionnaire, followed by wearable setup, the three-minute baseline calibration, and a short tutorial covering movement, roles, support actions, and the interface for the first assigned condition. The team then completed three mission blocks, one per condition, each followed by a post-condition questionnaire; a short break between blocks allowed sensor reliability checks. The session closed with a final comparison questionnaire in which participants ranked the three conditions and answered open-ended questions about usefulness, invasiveness, trust, confusion, and the control they wanted over their own physiological visibility.

\subsection{Measures}
The primary behavioral outcome was support response time, the seconds from a pressure event's onset to the first relevant support action by another teammate, because it directly tests whether physiological awareness helps players notice and respond to teammate strain. Secondary behavioral measures, logged automatically by the game client, included orders delivered, mission score, late and wrong orders, burned dishes, player idle time, collision/blocking count, task contribution balance, strategy changes, appropriate and false support actions, and recovery time. Team communication was recorded and coded post hoc for task instructions, help requests, support offers, status checks, physiological and numeric references, uncertainty statements, and strategy changes, to test whether the cue changes the balance between general uncertainty and targeted support (H2.6). After each condition, participants completed seven-point items on teammate awareness, coordination usefulness, and trust; the NASA Task Load Index for workload \cite{hart_development_1988}; the Self-Assessment Manikin for affective interpretation \cite{bradley_measuring_1994}, as in Study 1; selected Game Experience Questionnaire dimensions for player experience \cite{ijsselsteijn_game_2013}; and custom items on comfort sharing physiological data, invasiveness, and desired visibility control. Full item wording and the complete data-logging schema are reported in the supplementary materials described in Section~\ref{sec:data-availability}.

\subsection{Analysis Plan}
Behavioral outcomes are analyzed at the team-by-condition level with repeated-measures ANOVA where distributional assumptions hold and Friedman tests otherwise, with planned contrasts comparing the Icon Cue against the No Cue condition, the Icon Cue against the Numeric Cue condition, and the Numeric Cue against the No Cue condition; the primary planned contrast is Icon Cue versus No Cue on support response time. Questionnaire ratings are analyzed at the participant-by-condition level with repeated-measures ANOVA or linear mixed-effects models accounting for team membership. Coded communication frequencies are compared across conditions at the team-by-condition level, and physiological and interface logs are summarized descriptively to report sensor latency, missing-data rate, and state-display agreement as evidence for RQ2.4.

\subsection{Ethics Note for Reviewers}
All participants provide informed consent before participation and are told that HR and HRV data are collected for research and interface-visualization purposes only, not for medical diagnosis or emotional classification, and that the displayed states are non-diagnostic and non-emotional. Participants are assigned anonymous identifiers, physiological data are stored separately from identifying information, and participants may withdraw or stop physiological sharing at any time without penalty. Reported results use aggregate statistics and interface-state summaries wherever possible.

\section{Study 2 Results}
\label{sec:study2-results}
Study 2 data collection follows the design in Section~\ref{sec:study2-methods}. Behavioral logs, communication coding, and post-condition questionnaire results were not yet analyzed at the time of writing this manuscript. This section will report support response time and the other behavioral measures at the team-by-condition level, questionnaire outcomes at the participant-by-condition level, and the reliability of the wearable-to-interface pipeline (RQ2.4), alongside tests of H2.1--H2.6, in a subsequent revision once data collection and analysis are complete.

\section{Discussion}
\label{sec:discussion}

Study 1 set out to learn whether a teammate's body could be made readable at a distance without naming a feeling. The recognition scores and the five trend tests answer that question in the affirmative and let us say more precisely what such a display does and does not do; the subsections below discuss those findings, and a closing subsection turns to what Study 2 (Section~\ref{sec:study2-methods}) adds once its own results are analyzed.

\subsection{Legibility Without Emotional Labels}
The central result is that an interface can withhold every emotion word and still be read, well above chance even if not to ceiling. Participants recovered the heart rate state and the variability state from animation alone at 66.3 and 58.2 percent against a 25 percent chance level, and their readings ordered themselves with the underlying states across all five planned tests. This matters because it separates two things the shared physiology literature has tended to bundle. Systems that name an emotion assume the naming is warranted \cite{picard_affective_1997, mandryk_fuzzy_2007}, and systems that report warmer feeling between partners rarely check that the viewer can identify the state on show \cite{slovak_understanding_2012, hirsch_my_2023, hassan_augmenting_2024}. Prior ambiguous biosignal displays, closest among them Howell et al.'s skin-conductance work \cite{howell_biosignals_2016}, established that withholding a label does not prevent interpretation; our contribution is narrower and more quantitative: a label free display of cardiovascular state is legible to a reader at a measured, chance-referenced rate, and legible in a graded rather than a categorical way, even though the moderate accuracy and the confusion concentrated on adjacent middle states signal that legibility is not free of cost. The design withholds the verdict yet still communicates, which is the outcome the affective ambiguity argument predicts but had not tested at the level of identification accuracy \cite{howell_biosignals_2016, howell_tensions_2018}.

\subsection{Two Separable Physiological Channels}
Almost all prior work in this space transmits heart rate on its own. We encoded heart rate and heart rate variability as two visual channels, pulse and brightness against ring coherence, and the two were read as distinct social properties. Activation drove urgency and arousal, with each step raising perceived urgency by 0.40 points, while fragmentation drove a different axis, lowering perceived stability by 0.46 points per step and raising the intention to help. The two indices are physiologically distinct, since rate reflects momentary activation and variability reflects the autonomic regulation beneath it \cite{thayer_model_2000, porges_polyvagal_2007, shaffer_overview_2017}, and the study shows this distinction survives the translation into a single icon, even though the trend slopes are shallower than a fully confident, ceiling-level reading would produce. Performance held across all sixteen combinations rather than collapsing where an urgent pulse met a broken ring, though joint accuracy dipped furthest where a mid-range pulse met the least visually distinctive, stable-variability ring, which indicates that participants read the channels compositionally rather than memorising whole icons, with the two hardest cells arising precisely where both channels sat in their least distinctive middle range. For designers of physiological interfaces, this is evidence that more than one bodily signal can be carried at once, provided each is mapped to a visual variable a viewer can isolate, though the middle of each channel's range deserves more visual separation than the endpoints.

\subsection{From Reading a State to Intending to Act}
A display meant for coordination has to do more than convey a state; it has to move a teammate toward a response. The variability channel did exactly that. As ring coherence broke down, the intention to offer support rose monotonically, from 3.30 to 4.74 overall, and from 3.00 to 5.06 at the extremes of the full condition grid. This links a physiological cue to a prosocial disposition rather than only to felt closeness, and it speaks to the coordination problem that motivates the work, where teammates who cannot read one another misjudge who needs help \cite{tan_role_2014, bisberg_communication_2025, leavitt_ping_2016}. The result suggests that a shared bodily signal can act as a lightweight call for coordination, not merely as a channel for empathy, which extends the connection oriented framing of earlier heart rate sharing systems \cite{hassib_heartchat_2017, janssen_intimate_2010, jarvela_augmented_2021}.

\subsection{Ambiguity as a Designed Resource}
The design treated openness as a feature and grounded that decision in Science and Technology Studies. Because measurement helps constitute what it appears to record \cite{nelson_meeting_1996}, because the categories an interface fixes carry consequences \cite{bowker_sorting_2008, winner_artifacts_1980}, and because any reading of a body is situated \cite{haraway_situated_1988}, the interface offered graded cues to be interpreted in place rather than settled verdicts. The qualitative responses show this reasoning reached the participants: several valued that the display showed a pattern rather than a diagnosis, and read each state against the imagined match rather than as a fixed label. The theoretical contribution is therefore twofold. We offer a method that turns abstract STS commitments into concrete visual choices, and we provide evidence that the resulting artifact communicates, which moves the ambiguity argument from a critical stance into a validated design position \cite{howell_biosignals_2016, wakkary_things_2021}.

\subsection{Design Implications}
Four implications follow for physiological interfaces in shared settings. First, encoding relative to a personal baseline, rather than against an absolute scale, kept states legible without asserting a clinical reading, and is a defensible default for social displays. Second, separable visual variables allow a single icon to carry two bodily signals, which suggests headroom for richer physiological communication than the single heart rate channel now common. Third, withholding emotion labels still produced a legible, well above chance display and gained user acceptance, which indicates that graded, open cues are a viable alternative to categorical affect display, provided designers budget for a moderate accuracy ceiling rather than assuming legibility is automatic. Fourth, and most consequential, control over visibility is a precondition rather than a refinement, since participants accepted the display only when it remained optional, mutable, and subtle.

\subsection{Making the Body Visible Redistributes Accountability}
The boundaries participants drew are themselves a finding. Making a bodily signal visible changes the informational terms of play, and with them how blame, hesitation, and effort are read among teammates, which is precisely the redistribution of accountability that a politics of artifacts anticipates \cite{winner_artifacts_1980}. Participants asked for consent over who can see their status and for the ability to withdraw it, which recasts privacy from an afterthought into part of the interface's core logic. A deployed version would need to treat the right to be illegible as seriously as the capacity to be read.

\subsection{From Legibility to Coordination}
Every claim above concerns whether the icon communicates, not whether communicating it changes what a team does together. Study 2 (Section~\ref{sec:study2-methods}) is designed to close exactly that gap: it implements the acquisition, classification, and rendering architecture proposed in Section~\ref{sec:study1-methods} as a working multiplayer prototype, embeds it in an Overcooked-style cooperative task, and tests the Icon Cue against a No Cue baseline and an explicit Numeric Cue across scripted teammate-pressure events. Where Study 1's evidence is necessarily about interpretation in isolation, Study 2's design targets support response time, recovery time, coordination usefulness, workload, and privacy during live play, and its three-condition structure directly tests whether withholding numeric values costs or gains anything relative to explicit disclosure, a comparison Study 1 could not make (Section~\ref{sec:limitations}). Its behavioral, questionnaire, and communication-coding results were not yet analyzed at the time of writing (Section~\ref{sec:study2-results}); once available, they will show whether the graded legibility documented here also moves teams to notice and support one another faster, not only to read a state correctly when asked.

\section{Limitations}
\label{sec:limitations}

The limitations below bound the claims of Study 1 specifically; Study 2 (Section~\ref{sec:study2-methods}) was designed to close two of them directly, though its own results were not yet analyzed at the time of writing (Section~\ref{sec:study2-results}). Most importantly, Study 1 has no comparison condition: we never tested the label free, graded encoding against a numeric HR/HRV readout or against a display that names an emotion, so we cannot claim that withholding labels and using ambiguity as a design resource outperforms, or is even equivalent to, a simpler alternative on legibility, workload, or acceptance. The chance-referenced accuracy figures in Table~\ref{tab:legibility-tests} establish that the display communicates something, not that it communicates better than a display we did not build; a controlled comparison across encodings is necessary before that stronger claim can be made. Study 2's three-condition design, contrasting the Icon Cue against a No Cue baseline and an explicit Numeric Cue, is built to supply exactly that comparison, though only once its behavioral and questionnaire data are analyzed. Accuracy, while well above chance on every comparison, was moderate rather than near ceiling: HR recognition reached 66.3 percent and HRV recognition 58.2 percent, joint recognition reached 44.6 percent, and the middle states on each channel, especially stable HRV (V2) paired with a mid-range heart rate, were reliably harder to separate than the endpoints. A deployed system should budget for a comparable, and possibly higher, error rate rather than assume the four-state resolution used here is free of confusion, and a smaller state set concentrated on more separable endpoints may be preferable where a mistaken read carries a real coordination cost. The study validated interpretation of the visual language, not the fidelity of live capture or behaviour during play. Stimuli presented simulated cardiovascular states rather than signals streamed from a worn device, so the results establish that the icons are readable, not that a real Apple Watch feed would produce equally clean states under motion and noise; the streaming pipeline needed for that test is established but was not exercised here \cite{liang_harnessing_2024}. Reading icons in a status panel also differs from reading them under the attentional demand of a live match, and although workload stayed on the low side of the scale in isolation, it may rise further when the display competes with gameplay \cite{tu_biofeedback_2025}. The design is also tied to one vendor's sensing stack: it assumes every player wears an Apple Watch, which is not true of a general multiplayer audience spread across PC, console, and mixed device ecosystems. A deployed version needs a fallback for players without a supported wearable, most simply a neutral, unanimated avatar, and needs to generalise the baseline relative classification to other consumer sensors, whose photoplethysmography accuracy and HRV estimation vary by device.

The design was evaluated in a single online session, so retention, habituation, and whether the cue helps or distracts over extended play remain open even after Study 2, whose missions are also short. Study 1 measured legibility and the intention to act, not coordination outcomes; it does not show that teams using the display make faster or better decisions, which is precisely what Study 2's scripted teammate-pressure events and support-response-time measure are designed to test during real cooperative play (Section~\ref{sec:study2-methods}), pending the analysis noted in Section~\ref{sec:study2-results}. The sample of 36 participants was a convenience sample drawn from the author's university subject pool and personal network, recruited without compensation; it skewed toward ages 18 to 34, was self selected, and completed the task mostly on desktop, so both the lack of a probability sample and the lack of payment limit generalisation to broader, more diverse, or more incentivised populations, and to phone or in headset contexts. All ratings were self reported, without a physiological ground truth for the viewer, and the interpretation of heart rate variability is context dependent, so the relative states necessarily simplify a signal that is noisier in the field. Finally, the privacy concerns participants raised were surfaced qualitatively rather than designed for and evaluated, and a real deployment would need to resolve consent, data ownership, and control over visibility before these findings could be carried into practice.
\section{Conclusion}
Distributed play removes the body from social interaction. Teammates who would read one another's effort and strain at a glance in a shared room are left, across a network, inferring those states from text, voice, and avatar motion, and the resulting misreadings seed misplaced blame and broken coordination. This paper asked whether the missing bodily layer could be returned as a readable cue without collapsing it into a named emotion, and it answered in two stages: by designing and validating a peripheral avatar display, and by implementing that display in a live multiplayer prototype to test whether legibility translates into coordination.

Study 1, an online study with 36 participants, showed that the display communicates. Participants recovered the heart rate state and the variability state from animation alone at 66.3 and 58.2 percent, well above the 25 percent expected by chance though short of ceiling, and their readings ordered themselves with the underlying states across five planned trend tests: activation was read as urgency and arousal, declining ring coherence as lower stability, and fragmentation as a stronger call to help. The display's workload stayed on the low side of the scale and it was judged useful for cooperative play, and it did all of this while withholding every emotion word, which turns the argument that biosignals are ambiguous social cues from a critical stance into a validated, if moderately legible, design position.

Study 2 (Section~\ref{sec:study2-methods}) carries that validated icon system into a custom Overcooked-style cooperative task, implementing the acquisition, baseline-relative classification, and avatar-rendering architecture that Study 1 proposed but did not build, and testing it in 12 three-player teams against a no-cue baseline and an explicit numeric display across scripted teammate-pressure events. Its behavioral, questionnaire, and communication-coding results were not yet analyzed at the time of writing and will be reported in a subsequent revision (Section~\ref{sec:study2-results}); once available, they will speak directly to whether the graded legibility documented in Study 1 also changes teammate awareness, support timing, and coordination during real cooperative play, rather than only interpretation in isolation.

The contribution is fourfold: a wearable driven interaction technique that makes a distant teammate legible in a shared interactive space; a method that translates Science and Technology Studies commitments about measurement, classification, and situated knowledge into concrete visual choices; empirical evidence, from Study 1, that a label free physiological display is legible, graded, and light enough to use; and an implemented, live-multiplayer instantiation of that display, evaluated against a numeric-disclosure alternative in Study 2. The boundaries participants placed on physiological visibility in Study 1 are part of the result, and they set the design priority for Study 2's live implementation: to build consent and control over visibility into the interface's core so that the right to be illegible is held as firmly as the capacity to be read.
\section{Data and Materials Availability}
\label{sec:data-availability}
The anonymized trial-level dataset from Study 1 (participant demographics, Phase A and Phase B responses, and closing-questionnaire ratings), the stimulus animations, and the analysis scripts used to produce the statistics and figures in this paper will be deposited in a public repository upon publication. Study 2's materials, including the full post-condition questionnaire item bank, the communication-coding scheme, and the behavioral and physiological data-logging schema, together with its behavioral, questionnaire, and communication-coding datasets and analysis scripts, will be deposited alongside them once data collection and analysis are complete.

\section{Generative AI Use Disclosure}
A generative AI tool (ChatGPT) was used for language editing and restructuring of text drafted by the author across both studies reported in this paper; it was not used to generate the research idea, conceptual framework, study designs, stimuli, data, statistical results, or citations, and it played no role in participant recruitment, data collection, or analysis for either study. The author reviewed and takes responsibility for all content produced with its assistance.
